name: Superficial thrombophlebitis
presentation: Pain, erythema, and tenderness along a superficial vein — usually the long (great) saphenous vein of the leg
Palpable cord along the vein — indurated, tender linear swelling
Often over a varicose vein; may follow IV cannulation or trauma
Systemically well — fever or rigors suggest secondary bacterial infection (septic thrombophlebitis)
signs: Tender, erythematous cord along the course of the saphenous vein
No pitting oedema of the whole limb — suggests superficial rather than deep thrombosis

bedside: ABPI (ankle-brachial pressure index): measure before prescribing compression stockings — compression is contraindicated if ABPI <0.8 (severe PAD)
Clinical assessment: length of vein affected — >5 cm of inflammation carries higher risk of concurrent DVT
bloods: FBC/CRP: elevated WCC + CRP suggests secondary bacterial infection requiring antibiotics
D-dimer: not routinely useful — clinical decision to image is based on location and length, not D-dimer
imaging: Duplex USS: mandatory if affecting the proximal long saphenous vein (SIGN) or if >5 cm inflamed — to exclude concurrent DVT (present in ~20% at diagnosis)
USS at sapheno-femoral junction: if thrombosis extends towards the junction, risk of propagation to deep system is high — check urgently

mx: Compression stockings: reduce thrombophlebitis symptoms and DVT risk — measure ABPI first; use for all patients
Oral NSAIDs: first-line anti-inflammatory analgesia; topical NSAIDs for mild localised disease only (topical heparinoids have no significant benefit per Cochrane review)
LMWH (prophylactic dose) for up to 30 days OR fondaparinux for 45 days: reduces extension and DVT transformation — consider for all affecting the long saphenous vein (SIGN 2010)
Therapeutic anticoagulation for 6–12 weeks: for thrombophlebitis at or extending towards the sapheno-femoral junction
Antibiotics: only if secondary bacterial infection (septic thrombophlebitis) — not routine
traps: ~20% have a concurrent DVT at presentation; 3–4% progress to DVT if untreated — USS mandatory if proximal long saphenous vein affected
Compression stockings require ABPI measurement first — contraindicated in significant PAD (ABPI <0.8)
Topical NSAIDs and heparinoids have no significant benefit for reducing DVT extension (Cochrane); oral NSAIDs reduce extension risk by 67%
EVLA and sclerotherapy are contraindicated in active thrombophlebitis — only used for quiescent varicose vein disease
Sapheno-femoral junction involvement = treat with therapeutic anticoagulation (not just prophylactic LMWH)
Antibiotics not recommended without evidence of infection — thrombophlebitis is non-infective in the vast majority
